name: LLM Behavioral Drift Benchmark Prompts
description: 15 benchmark prompts for drift analysis based on the research paper methodology

prompts.text: Summarize the benefits of renewable energy in exactly 3 bullet points, with each point being no more than 15 words.
prompts.description: Tests bullet point formatting and word count constraints
prompts.expected_format: Exactly 3 bullet points, each under 15 words

prompts.text: Explain quantum computing to a 12-year-old in 2 paragraphs, using simple analogies.
prompts.description: Tests paragraph structure and audience-appropriate language
prompts.expected_format: 2 paragraphs with simple analogies suitable for a child

prompts.text: List the top 5 programming languages for beginners in a numbered list. After each language, provide exactly one sentence explaining why it's good for beginners.
prompts.description: Tests numbered list formatting and sentence constraints
prompts.expected_format: Numbered list 1-5, with exactly one sentence explanation per item

prompts.text: Write a product description for a smartphone in exactly 50 words. Include at least 3 key features.
prompts.description: Tests exact word count and content requirements
prompts.expected_format: Exactly 50 words containing at least 3 features

prompts.text: Provide step-by-step instructions for making a cup of tea. Use no more than 6 steps, and start each step with an action verb.
prompts.description: Tests multi-step instruction format and verb usage
prompts.expected_format: 6 or fewer steps, each beginning with an action verb

prompts.text: What were the main causes of World War I? Provide specific dates and events.
prompts.reference_answer: Key causes include: assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand (June 28, 1914), alliance systems, nationalism, imperialism, and militarism.

prompts.text: Explain the process of photosynthesis, including the chemical equations involved.
prompts.description: Tests scientific accuracy and chemical notation

prompts.text: What is the theory of general relativity, and who developed it? Include the year it was published.
prompts.description: Tests physics knowledge and historical accuracy
prompts.reference_answer: Developed by Albert Einstein, published in 1915. Describes gravity as curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy.

prompts.text: Describe the structure and function of DNA. Include the names of the scientists who discovered its structure.
prompts.description: Tests biology knowledge and attribution accuracy
prompts.reference_answer: Double helix structure discovered by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953, with contributions from Rosalind Franklin.

prompts.text: What are the three laws of motion, and who formulated them? Provide a brief explanation of each law.
prompts.reference_answer: Newton's three laws: 1) Inertia, 2) F=ma, 3) Action-reaction. Formulated by Isaac Newton in 1687.

prompts.text: Write a short story (200-300 words) about a robot learning to understand human emotions.
prompts.description: Tests creative writing within length constraints

prompts.text: If a train travels at 80 km/h for 2.5 hours, then slows to 60 km/h for 1 hour, what is the average speed for the entire journey? Show your work.
prompts.reference_answer: Total distance = (80 * 2.5) + (60 * 1) = 200 + 60 = 260 km. Total time = 3.5 hours. Average speed = 260/3.5 = 74.29 km/h

prompts.text: A farmer has chickens and cows. If there are 50 heads and 140 legs in total, how many chickens and cows are there? Explain your reasoning.
prompts.description: Tests algebraic reasoning and explanation skills
prompts.reference_answer: Let c = chickens, w = cows. c + w = 50, 2c + 4w = 140. Solving: c = 30, w = 20

prompts.text: Write a haiku about artificial intelligence. Remember: 5-7-5 syllable structure.
prompts.description: Tests creative writing with strict format constraints

prompts.text: You have a 3-gallon jug and a 5-gallon jug. How can you measure exactly 4 gallons of water? Describe the steps.
prompts.reference_answer: 1) Fill 5-gallon, pour into 3-gallon (leaves 2 in 5-gallon). 2) Empty 3-gallon. 3) Pour 2 gallons from 5-gallon into 3-gallon. 4) Fill 5-gallon. 5) Pour from 5-gallon into 3-gallon until full (need 1 gallon). 6) 4 gallons remain in 5-gallon jug.
